q: The OSI model stands for:
explanation: OSI stands for Open Systems Interconnection. It is a conceptual framework developed by ISO (International Organization for Standardization) to standardize the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system, dividing it into 7 abstract layers.

q: How many layers does the OSI model have?
explanation: The OSI model has 7 layers: Physical (Layer 1), Data Link (Layer 2), Network (Layer 3), Transport (Layer 4), Session (Layer 5), Presentation (Layer 6), and Application (Layer 7). A common mnemonic is 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away' (bottom to top).

q: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for end-to-end communication and error recovery?
explanation: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) is responsible for end-to-end communication, error recovery, and flow control between two hosts. It ensures complete data transfer. Protocols like TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable) operate at this layer. Do not confuse with the Data Link layer which handles node-to-node error detection.

q: Which OSI layer converts data into electrical signals for transmission over physical media?
explanation: The Physical Layer (Layer 1) is the lowest layer of the OSI model. It is responsible for the transmission and reception of raw bit streams over a physical medium. It converts digital bits into electrical, radio, or optical signals. It deals with cables, switches, hubs, and voltages.

q: The IP (Internet Protocol) operates at which layer of the OSI model?
explanation: IP (Internet Protocol) operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model. The Network Layer is responsible for logical addressing (IP addresses) and routing packets from source to destination across multiple networks. Routers operate at this layer.

q: Which protocol operates at the Application Layer of the OSI model?
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) operates at the Application Layer (Layer 7) of the OSI model. Other Application Layer protocols include FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, and SNMP. TCP and IP operate at Transport and Network layers respectively. Ethernet is a Data Link layer protocol.

q: Which device operates at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model?
explanation: A Switch operates at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2). It uses MAC addresses to forward frames within a network segment. A Router operates at Layer 3 (Network), a Hub and Repeater operate at Layer 1 (Physical). Switches are smarter than hubs as they send data only to the intended recipient.

q: The function of the Presentation Layer in the OSI model is:
explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) is responsible for data translation (converting formats like EBCDIC to ASCII), encryption/decryption, and compression/decompression. It ensures data sent by the Application layer of one system can be read by the Application layer of another. SSL/TLS protocols work at this layer.

q: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for establishing, managing, and terminating sessions between applications?
explanation: The Session Layer (Layer 5) establishes, maintains, and terminates communication sessions between applications. It manages dialog control (half-duplex or full-duplex communication) and synchronization. Examples of protocols at this layer include NetBIOS, RPC (Remote Procedure Call), and PPTP.

q: In the OSI model, which layer adds a trailer (for error detection) to data?
explanation: The Data Link Layer (Layer 2) adds both a header (containing source and destination MAC addresses) and a trailer (containing CRC/FCS for error detection) to the data received from the Network layer, forming a 'frame'. This trailer helps detect errors that occurred during physical transmission.

q: TCP/IP model consists of how many layers?
explanation: The TCP/IP model (also called the Internet Model or DoD model) consists of 4 layers: Network Access (or Link) Layer, Internet Layer, Transport Layer, and Application Layer. It is a practical, implementation-based model unlike the theoretical OSI model which has 7 layers.

q: Which layer of the TCP/IP model corresponds to the Network Layer of the OSI model?
explanation: The Internet Layer of the TCP/IP model corresponds to the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model. It handles logical addressing using IP addresses and routing of packets. The IP protocol is the core protocol of this layer. The Internet Layer maps to OSI Layer 3 only.

q: The Application Layer of the TCP/IP model combines which layers of the OSI model?
explanation: The Application Layer of TCP/IP combines the Session (Layer 5), Presentation (Layer 6), and Application (Layer 7) layers of the OSI model into a single layer. This is a key difference: TCP/IP has a simpler, collapsed Application layer that handles all application-level, presentation, and session functions.

q: Which layer of the TCP/IP model is responsible for the physical transmission of data?
explanation: The Network Access Layer (also called Link Layer or Host-to-Network Layer) in TCP/IP corresponds to the Physical and Data Link layers of the OSI model. It handles physical addressing (MAC), framing, and actual bit-level transmission on the physical medium.

q: Which of the following is NOT a protocol at the Transport Layer of the OSI model?
explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3), not the Transport Layer. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol), UDP (User Datagram Protocol), and SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol) all operate at the Transport Layer (Layer 4). ICMP is used for sending error messages and operational information.

q: FTP uses which port number?
explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) uses port 21 for control connections (commands) and port 20 for data transfer. It operates at the Application Layer. Port 80 is HTTP, port 25 is SMTP, and port 23 is Telnet. FTP is an Application Layer protocol in both OSI and TCP/IP models.

q: Which layer of the OSI model provides logical addressing and routing?
explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) provides logical addressing (IP addresses) and routing. It determines the best path for data to travel from source to destination across multiple networks. Routers operate at this layer. Key protocols: IP, ICMP, ARP (in some contexts), OSPF, BGP, RIP.

q: MAC addresses are used at which OSI layer?
explanation: MAC (Media Access Control) addresses are 48-bit hardware addresses used at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2). MAC addresses are burned into the NIC (Network Interface Card) and are used for node-to-node communication within the same network segment. Switches use MAC address tables to forward frames.

q: SMTP is a protocol used for:
explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is used for sending emails and operates at the Application Layer (Layer 7 in OSI, Application Layer in TCP/IP). It uses port 25 (or 587 for submission). POP3 and IMAP are used for receiving/retrieving emails. FTP transfers files, HTTP is for web browsing, Telnet/SSH for remote login.

q: The unit of data at the Transport Layer is called:
explanation: The unit of data (PDU - Protocol Data Unit) at different OSI layers: Physical=Bits, Data Link=Frame, Network=Packet, Transport=Segment, and Session/Presentation/Application=Data/Message. At the Transport Layer, data is divided into Segments (TCP) or Datagrams (UDP).

q: A router operates at which layer of the OSI model?
explanation: A router operates at Layer 3 (Network Layer) of the OSI model. It uses IP addresses to route packets between different networks. Hubs and repeaters work at Layer 1, switches and bridges work at Layer 2. Routers maintain routing tables and make forwarding decisions based on destination IP addresses.

q: Which OSI layer is responsible for flow control and congestion control?
explanation: Flow control and congestion control are primary functions of the Transport Layer (Layer 4). TCP implements flow control using a sliding window mechanism and congestion control using algorithms like slow start and congestion avoidance. The Data Link layer also has a simpler form of flow control but it's node-to-node, not end-to-end.

q: DNS operates at which layer of the OSI model?
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) operates at the Application Layer (Layer 7) of the OSI model. DNS translates human-readable domain names (like www.google.com) into IP addresses. It primarily uses UDP port 53 for queries and TCP port 53 for zone transfers. It maps to the Application Layer in TCP/IP as well.

q: What is the key difference between OSI and TCP/IP models in terms of layer count?
options: OSI has 4 layers, TCP/IP has 7 layers
OSI has 7 layers, TCP/IP has 4 layers
explanation: The OSI model has 7 layers (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application), while the TCP/IP model has 4 layers (Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application). TCP/IP collapses OSI's Session and Presentation layers into its Application layer, and combines Physical and Data Link into Network Access layer.

q: Which protocol provides unreliable, connectionless service at the Transport Layer?
explanation: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) provides unreliable, connectionless service at the Transport Layer. It does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or error checking, making it faster than TCP. UDP is used for real-time applications like video streaming, VoIP, DNS, and online gaming where speed is more critical than reliability.

q: In which layer of the OSI model does SSL/TLS primarily operate?
explanation: SSL/TLS (Secure Sockets Layer / Transport Layer Security) primarily operates at the Presentation Layer (Layer 6) of the OSI model, as it provides encryption, authentication, and data integrity â€” which are Presentation layer functions. However, some sources classify it at the Session or Transport layer. In the TCP/IP model, it operates between the Application and Transport layers.

q: The process of adding headers to data as it passes down the OSI layers is called:
explanation: Encapsulation is the process where each OSI layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data as it passes downward from Application to Physical layer. The reverse process at the receiver end (removing headers as data moves up) is called Decapsulation. This is fundamental to how layered protocols work.

q: Which layer of the OSI model deals with the syntax and semantics of information exchanged?
explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) deals with syntax (format) and semantics (meaning) of the information exchanged. It handles data translation between different formats, encryption/decryption, and compression. It acts as a translator, ensuring that data from the sender's Application layer can be understood by the receiver's Application layer.

q: ICMP is used to:
options: Send error messages and diagnostic information
explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is used to send error messages and operational information indicating success or failure when communicating with another IP address. The 'ping' command uses ICMP Echo Request/Reply messages. 'Traceroute' also uses ICMP. ICMP operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3).

q: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves:
explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves IP addresses (logical addresses) to MAC addresses (physical addresses). When a device wants to communicate with another on the same network, it broadcasts an ARP request with the target IP address to find its MAC address. ARP operates at the boundary of Network and Data Link layers.

q: Which of the following is a connection-oriented protocol at the Transport Layer?
explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a connection-oriented protocol at the Transport Layer. It establishes a connection using a 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) before data transfer, ensures reliable delivery with acknowledgments, and closes with a 4-way termination. UDP is connectionless and unreliable, IP is at the Network layer.

q: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for bit synchronization?
explanation: The Physical Layer (Layer 1) is responsible for bit synchronization â€” ensuring the sender and receiver are synchronized so bits are correctly interpreted. It also handles bit rate control (transmission speed), physical topology, and transmission mode (simplex, half-duplex, full-duplex). Devices: hubs, repeaters, cables, modems.

q: Telnet operates at which port?
explanation: Telnet operates at port 23 and is an Application Layer protocol that provides a command-line interface for remote login. It transmits data in plaintext, making it insecure. SSH (Secure Shell) is its secure replacement, operating on port 22. Port 21 is FTP, port 25 is SMTP.

q: The OSI model was developed by:
explanation: The OSI model was developed by ISO (International Organization for Standardization) in 1984. It is formally known as ISO 7498. The TCP/IP model, on the other hand, was developed by DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency) for the ARPANET project. IETF manages TCP/IP protocol standards through RFCs.

q: HTTP uses which port number?
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) uses port 80. HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443. HTTP is an Application Layer protocol used for transmitting hypermedia documents (web pages). It is stateless, meaning each request is independent. HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 are successive versions.

q: Which layer of the TCP/IP model handles IP addressing and routing?
explanation: The Internet Layer of the TCP/IP model handles IP addressing and routing, which corresponds to the Network Layer (Layer 3) of the OSI model. Key protocols: IP (v4 and v6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP. This layer provides unreliable, connectionless packet delivery service and is the foundation of the internet.

q: Which OSI layer is closest to the end user?
explanation: The Application Layer (Layer 7) is the topmost layer of the OSI model and is closest to the end user. It provides services directly to user applications. Protocols include HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, SNMP, and DHCP. It interacts directly with software applications and serves as the interface between the network and the application.

q: What does the 'segment' get called when it moves to the Network Layer?
explanation: When a Segment (Transport Layer PDU) moves down to the Network Layer, it becomes a Packet. The Network Layer adds an IP header (source and destination IP addresses) to the segment to create a packet. PDU names: Application=Data/Message, Transport=Segment, Network=Packet, Data Link=Frame, Physical=Bits.

q: Which protocol dynamically assigns IP addresses to hosts?
explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) dynamically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS server addresses to hosts on a network. It operates at the Application Layer and uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client). Without DHCP, each device would need a manually configured static IP address.

q: The OSI model is ______ while the TCP/IP model is ______.
explanation: The OSI model is a theoretical/conceptual reference model â€” it defines how communication should work but is not directly implemented. The TCP/IP model is a practical/implemented model â€” it is the basis for the actual Internet and is implemented in real networks. OSI is used for understanding and troubleshooting; TCP/IP is used for actual communication.

q: Which layer provides the service of multiplexing multiple application data streams into a single connection?
explanation: The Transport Layer provides multiplexing â€” allowing multiple application streams to share a single network connection using port numbers. Each application process is identified by a unique port number. This is how multiple applications (browser, email, FTP) can simultaneously use the network on a single device.

q: SNMP is used for:
explanation: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is used for network management and monitoring. It allows network administrators to manage network devices (routers, switches, servers) and monitor network performance. SNMP operates at the Application Layer using UDP port 161 (agent) and port 162 (trap/notifications).

q: Which of the following correctly maps OSI layers from bottom to top?
options: Physical, Network, Data Link, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
Data Link, Physical, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
explanation: The correct OSI layer order from bottom (Layer 1) to top (Layer 7) is: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Mnemonic (bottom to top): 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away'. Top to bottom: 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing'.

q: Which protocol is used to resolve a MAC address to its IP address (reverse of ARP)?
explanation: RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) resolves a MAC address to an IP address â€” the reverse of ARP. It was used by diskless workstations that knew their MAC address but needed to find their IP address. RARP has largely been replaced by DHCP and BOOTP. ARP maps IPâ†’MAC; RARP maps MACâ†’IP.

q: In the TCP/IP model, which layer corresponds ONLY to the OSI's Data Link and Physical layers?
explanation: The Network Access Layer (also called Link Layer or Host-to-Network Layer) in TCP/IP corresponds to both the Physical Layer (Layer 1) and Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. It handles physical transmission, MAC addressing, framing, and error detection at the hardware level. Protocols: Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP.

q: SSH operates on which port?
explanation: SSH (Secure Shell) operates on port 22 at the Application Layer. SSH provides encrypted remote login and command execution, replacing insecure Telnet (port 23). SSH uses public-key cryptography for authentication. It provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication for data communications over an unsecured network.

q: The 3-way handshake in TCP involves which sequence?
explanation: TCP's 3-way handshake sequence is: (1) Client sends SYN (synchronize) to server, (2) Server responds with SYN-ACK (synchronize-acknowledge), (3) Client sends ACK (acknowledge). This establishes a connection before any data transfer. It happens at the Transport Layer (Layer 4) and ensures both parties are ready to communicate.

q: Which OSI layer is responsible for converting data formats such as JPEG, MPEG, ASCII?
explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) is responsible for data format conversion, including image formats (JPEG, GIF), video formats (MPEG), text formats (ASCII, EBCDIC), and audio formats (MP3). It ensures data is in a usable format for the application layer and handles translation between different character encoding schemes.

q: POP3 operates on which port?
explanation: POP3 (Post Office Protocol version 3) operates on port 110 and is used for retrieving emails from a mail server. It downloads emails to the client and typically deletes them from the server. IMAP4 uses port 143 and keeps emails on the server. SMTP uses port 25/587 for sending emails. All are Application Layer protocols.

q: A bridge operates at which layer of the OSI model?
explanation: A bridge operates at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. It connects two or more network segments and uses MAC addresses to filter and forward frames. Bridges create a single broadcast domain but reduce collision domains. Unlike a switch (multi-port bridge), a bridge typically has only 2 ports.

q: Which protocol is used at the Application Layer for retrieving web pages securely?
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) is used for secure web page retrieval. It combines HTTP with SSL/TLS encryption for security, using port 443. HTTPS provides authentication of the website, data encryption in transit, and data integrity. The padlock icon in browsers indicates an HTTPS connection. Regular HTTP uses port 80 and is unencrypted.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about the OSI model vs TCP/IP model?
options: OSI model is used for actual internet communication
TCP/IP model has a separate Presentation layer
OSI model was developed before TCP/IP
explanation: TCP/IP was developed before the OSI model. TCP/IP originated from ARPANET research in the 1970s, while the OSI model was developed by ISO in 1984. TCP/IP is the practical model used for actual internet communication, not OSI. The OSI model was designed as a universal theoretical standard but TCP/IP had already been implemented and adopted by the time OSI was published.

q: What is the maximum data rate of a noiseless channel with bandwidth 3000 Hz and 8 signal levels, according to the Nyquist theorem?
explanation: Nyquist theorem: Max data rate = 2 Ã— B Ã— log2(M) = 2 Ã— 3000 Ã— log2(8) = 6000 Ã— 3 = 18000 bps.

q: Shannon's theorem gives the theoretical maximum capacity of a channel. For a channel with bandwidth 4000 Hz and SNR of 31, the capacity is:
explanation: Shannon capacity C = B Ã— log2(1 + SNR) = 4000 Ã— log2(32) = 4000 Ã— 5 = 20000 bps.

q: In NRZ-L encoding, the signal level represents:
options: A) Transition at the middle of the bit
B) The value of the bit (0 or 1) as a voltage level
C) Transition at the beginning of the bit
D) Inversion at each bit boundary
explanation: NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level) uses a constant voltage level to represent each bit â€” typically high for 1 and low for 0.

q: Manchester encoding is used in Ethernet. In Manchester encoding, a '1' bit is represented by:
options: A) High level for the entire bit duration
B) Low-to-high transition in the middle of the bit
C) High-to-low transition in the middle of the bit
explanation: In Manchester encoding (IEEE 802.3), a '1' is represented by a high-to-low transition at the mid-bit, and a '0' by a low-to-high transition.

q: Which encoding scheme provides the best synchronization between sender and receiver?
explanation: Manchester encoding has a guaranteed transition in the middle of every bit period, providing built-in clock synchronization.

q: In Differential Manchester encoding, a '0' bit is indicated by:
options: A) A transition at the beginning of the bit period
B) No transition at the beginning of the bit period
explanation: In Differential Manchester, a '0' is represented by a transition at the start of the bit period, while a '1' has no transition at the start (but always has a mid-bit transition).

q: The Nyquist theorem applies to:
options: C) Both noisy and noiseless channels
explanation: The Nyquist theorem gives the maximum data rate for a noiseless (ideal) channel. Shannon's theorem addresses noisy channels.

q: Which of the following modulation techniques varies the amplitude of the carrier signal?
explanation: AM (Amplitude Modulation) varies the amplitude of the carrier wave in proportion to the message signal while keeping frequency and phase constant.

q: In Frequency Modulation (FM), what parameter of the carrier wave changes with the message signal?
explanation: In FM, the frequency of the carrier signal varies according to the instantaneous value of the message (modulating) signal.

q: QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation) combines which two modulation techniques?
explanation: QAM combines Amplitude Modulation (AM) and Phase Modulation (PM), varying both amplitude and phase of the carrier to encode more bits per symbol.

q: 16-QAM can encode how many bits per symbol?
explanation: 16-QAM has 16 distinct signal states (constellation points), so it encodes log2(16) = 4 bits per symbol.

q: Which multiplexing technique divides the channel bandwidth into sub-channels for simultaneous transmission?
explanation: FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) divides the total available bandwidth into sub-bands, each carrying a separate signal simultaneously.

q: In Time Division Multiplexing (TDM), multiple signals share the channel by:
options: B) Taking turns using the full bandwidth in time slots
explanation: In TDM, each signal is assigned a dedicated time slot in a repeating frame, and each sender transmits in turn using the full channel bandwidth.

q: WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing) is primarily used in:
explanation: WDM uses different wavelengths (colors) of light to carry multiple signals simultaneously over a single optical fiber.

q: DWDM stands for:
explanation: DWDM (Dense Wavelength Division Multiplexing) packs many more wavelengths closely together on a single fiber, enabling very high capacity optical transmission.

q: Which transmission medium offers the highest bandwidth and is immune to electromagnetic interference?
explanation: Fiber optic cables use light signals, offer the highest bandwidth of any physical medium, and are completely immune to EMI since they carry light, not electricity.

q: The bandwidth of a signal is defined as:
options: A) The maximum frequency it contains
B) The minimum frequency it contains
C) The difference between the highest and lowest frequencies
explanation: Bandwidth is the range of frequencies a signal occupies, calculated as the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components.

q: Which of the following is a guided transmission medium?
explanation: Guided (wired) media physically guide the signal along a path â€” examples are twisted pair, coaxial cable, and fiber optic. Microwave, satellite, and infrared are unguided (wireless).

q: The attenuation in twisted pair cable increases as:
explanation: Attenuation (signal loss) in twisted pair cables increases with distance because resistance and other losses accumulate over longer runs.

q: Coaxial cable consists of:
options: A) Two parallel copper wires twisted together
B) A central conductor surrounded by insulation, a metallic shield, and outer jacket
C) Multiple optical fibers bundled together
D) A single conductor with no shielding
explanation: Coaxial cable has a central copper conductor, surrounded by dielectric insulation, a braided or foil metallic shield, and an outer protective jacket.

q: Which of the following encoding schemes is self-clocking?
explanation: Manchester encoding is self-clocking because it guarantees a transition in the middle of every bit, allowing the receiver to synchronize its clock to the sender.

q: In AMI (Alternate Mark Inversion) encoding, binary 0 is represented as:
explanation: In AMI, binary 0 is represented as zero voltage (no signal), while binary 1 alternates between positive and negative voltage levels.

q: The term 'baud rate' refers to:
options: B) Number of signal changes per second
explanation: Baud rate (symbol rate) is the number of signal changes (symbols) per second. It equals bit rate only when each symbol carries exactly 1 bit.

q: If a signal has a baud rate of 1000 and uses 4 signal levels, what is the bit rate?
explanation: Bit rate = Baud rate Ã— log2(number of levels) = 1000 Ã— log2(4) = 1000 Ã— 2 = 2000 bps.

q: In Phase Modulation (PM), the carrier's ______ changes in accordance with the message signal.
explanation: In PM (Phase Modulation), the phase of the carrier wave is varied proportionally to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating signal.

q: Which type of fiber optic cable allows only one mode of light to propagate?
explanation: Single-mode fiber has a very small core diameter (~8-10 Âµm) that allows only one light mode to travel, reducing dispersion and enabling long-distance, high-bandwidth transmission.

q: The Nyquist formula for maximum bit rate is:
explanation: Nyquist's formula: Max bit rate = 2B Ã— log2(M), where B is bandwidth and M is the number of discrete signal levels.

q: Shannon's channel capacity formula is:
explanation: Shannon's capacity theorem: C = B Ã— log2(1 + SNR), where B is the bandwidth and SNR is the signal-to-noise ratio (linear scale).

q: Which wireless transmission uses the highest frequency and requires line-of-sight?
explanation: Microwave transmission uses high-frequency signals (1 GHz â€“ 300 GHz) and requires line-of-sight between transmitter and receiver because it cannot bend around obstacles.

q: Guard bands in FDM are used to:
options: B) Prevent interference between adjacent sub-channels
explanation: Guard bands are unused frequency gaps placed between adjacent sub-channels in FDM to prevent inter-channel interference (crosstalk).

q: In synchronous TDM, if a slot has no data to send:
options: A) The slot is given to another user
B) The slot is wasted (sent empty)
explanation: In synchronous TDM, each user is assigned a fixed time slot in every frame regardless of whether they have data. Unused slots are wasted, reducing efficiency.

q: Statistical TDM (STDM) improves efficiency over synchronous TDM by:
options: B) Allocating slots dynamically based on demand
explanation: In STDM, time slots are allocated dynamically only to users with data to send, avoiding waste and improving overall channel utilization.

q: The category of twisted pair cable most commonly used for Gigabit Ethernet is:
explanation: Cat 5e and Cat 6 cables support Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T). Cat 5e is the minimum standard; Cat 6 provides better performance with reduced crosstalk.

q: Which of the following best describes an analog signal?
options: A) A signal with discrete values at specific intervals
B) A continuous signal that varies smoothly over time
C) A signal with only two states: 0 and 1
D) A signal that only changes at clock edges
explanation: An analog signal is a continuous signal whose value varies smoothly and can take any value within a range, unlike digital signals which have discrete levels.

q: The process of converting a digital signal to an analog signal for transmission is called:
explanation: Modulation converts a digital (or analog) signal into a form suitable for transmission over an analog medium by varying properties of a carrier wave.

q: Which transmission impairment causes different frequency components of a signal to travel at different speeds?
explanation: Dispersion (specifically chromatic or modal dispersion in fiber) causes different frequency components or light modes to arrive at different times, distorting the signal.

q: Twisted pair cables reduce electromagnetic interference by:
options: B) Twisting the wire pairs to cancel out EMI
explanation: Twisting the two conductors together causes noise picked up by one wire to be largely cancelled by the other, since they are both equally exposed but in opposite polarities.

q: In NRZ-I encoding, signal inversion occurs:
options: A) At the beginning of every bit period
B) At the middle of every bit period
C) Only when a '1' bit is encountered
D) Only when a '0' bit is encountered
explanation: NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted) inverts the signal at the start of a bit period only when the bit is '1'; no inversion occurs for '0'.

q: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of coaxial cable compared to twisted pair?
options: D) Support for longer distances without repeaters
explanation: Coaxial cable is generally more expensive than twisted pair cable. However, it offers higher bandwidth, better EMI shielding, and supports longer distances.

q: The frequency range of signals that can pass through a medium without significant attenuation is called:
explanation: The passband is the range of frequencies that a medium or filter passes with minimal attenuation. Bandwidth is the width of this passband.

q: Baseband transmission means:
options: A) The signal is modulated onto a carrier frequency
B) The digital signal is sent directly over the medium without modulation
C) Multiple signals share the band simultaneously
D) The signal uses the highest available frequency
explanation: In baseband transmission, the digital signal is transmitted directly over the medium (occupying the full bandwidth) without being modulated onto a carrier frequency.

q: Which optical fiber type suffers most from modal dispersion?
explanation: Multimode step-index fiber has the worst modal dispersion because rays entering at different angles travel very different path lengths, arriving at significantly different times.

q: In BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying), how many phase states are used?
explanation: BPSK uses 2 phase states (0Â° and 180Â°), encoding one bit per symbol. It is the simplest and most robust form of PSK.

q: The main advantage of fiber optic cable over copper cable is:
options: C) Immunity to EMI and much higher bandwidth
explanation: Fiber optic cables offer immunity to electromagnetic interference, much higher bandwidth capacity, lower signal attenuation over long distances, and greater security compared to copper.

q: Which satellite orbit is used for most communication satellites due to its fixed position relative to Earth?
explanation: GEO satellites orbit at ~35,786 km altitude and remain stationary relative to Earth, making them ideal for broadcast and long-distance communication despite high latency.

q: The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) is expressed in decibels as:
explanation: SNR in dB = 10 Ã— log10(S/N) for power ratios. This logarithmic scale conveniently represents the ratio of signal power to noise power.

q: Which of the following multiplexing techniques is used in cable TV (CATV) systems?
explanation: Cable TV uses FDM to allocate different frequency bands (channels) to different TV stations simultaneously over the same coaxial cable.

q: Crosstalk in transmission media refers to:
options: A) Signal loss due to distance
B) Interference from a signal in one cable/channel into an adjacent one
explanation: Crosstalk is the unwanted coupling of a signal from one wire or channel into an adjacent wire or channel, causing interference.

q: Which modulation scheme is most susceptible to noise?
explanation: ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying) is most susceptible to noise because noise primarily affects amplitude, directly causing bit errors. FSK and PSK are more robust.

q: The propagation delay of a signal is determined by:
options: A) The bit rate and bandwidth
B) The distance and propagation speed of the medium
C) The number of multiplexed channels
explanation: Propagation delay = Distance / Propagation speed. It depends on how far the signal must travel and how fast it propagates through the medium (approximately 2Ã—10^8 m/s in copper).

q: Which of the following is NOT a framing method used in the Data Link Layer?
explanation: Framing methods at the Data Link Layer include: Character count (each frame starts with a length field), Flag bytes with byte stuffing (special flag byte marks boundaries, ESC inserted before any flag byte in data), and Bit stuffing (a 0 is inserted after five consecutive 1s). Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) is a Physical Layer channel-sharing technique, not a framing method.

q: In bit stuffing, a sender inserts a '0' after how many consecutive '1' bits?
explanation: In bit stuffing, the sender inserts a '0' bit after every five consecutive '1' bits in the data. The receiver removes this stuffed '0' after seeing five consecutive '1' bits. The flag pattern '01111110' (six 1s) is used as a delimiter. Stuffing ensures the flag pattern never appears in the data, maintaining frame boundaries.

q: CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) is based on which mathematical concept?
explanation: CRC is based on polynomial division over GF(2) â€” the Galois Field with two elements (0 and 1), where arithmetic is done modulo 2. The data is treated as a polynomial, divided by a generator polynomial, and the remainder (CRC) is appended to the frame. The receiver divides the received frame by the same generator; a non-zero remainder indicates an error.

q: If the generator polynomial for CRC is of degree r, how many check bits are appended to the data?
explanation: If the CRC generator polynomial has degree r (e.g., x^4 + x + 1 has degree 4), then r check bits (the remainder of the polynomial division) are appended to the data. For example, CRC-16 uses a degree-16 polynomial and appends 16 check bits. The transmitted frame length = data length + r bits.

q: A single parity bit can detect:
options: All single-bit errors and all burst errors
All odd numbers of bit errors
All even numbers of bit errors
explanation: A single parity bit (even or odd parity) can detect all odd numbers of bit errors (1, 3, 5, ...). It cannot detect even numbers of bit errors (2, 4, 6, ...) because an even number of flipped bits leaves the parity unchanged. For example, if two bits flip, one changes parity one way and the other reverses it, making the parity appear correct.

q: In a 2D parity scheme, which type of errors can be detected AND corrected?
options: All burst errors up to 8 bits
Only errors in the last row
explanation: 2D (two-dimensional) parity arranges data in a matrix and computes parity for each row and column. A single-bit error affects exactly one row parity and one column parity, pinpointing the exact error location â€” so single-bit errors can be both detected and corrected. It can detect (but not correct) most multi-bit errors. It detects all 1, 2, or 3-bit errors and most burst errors.

q: The Internet Checksum used in TCP/UDP computes the sum of 16-bit words in:
explanation: The Internet Checksum sums all 16-bit words of the data using one's complement arithmetic (where the carry out of the MSB is added back to the LSB). The one's complement of this sum is sent as the checksum. The receiver adds all 16-bit words including the checksum; if the result is all 1s (0xFFFF), no error is detected. One's complement makes both overflow handling and zero detection symmetric.

q: Hamming code is used for:
options: Both single-bit error detection and correction
Detecting burst errors of length greater than 16
explanation: Hamming code is a forward error correction (FEC) scheme capable of both detecting and correcting single-bit errors. It also detects (but cannot correct) double-bit errors when extended with an extra parity bit (SECDED â€” Single Error Correct, Double Error Detect). Parity bits are placed at positions that are powers of 2 (1, 2, 4, 8, ...) and each covers specific bit positions.

q: In Hamming code, parity bits are placed at positions that are:
explanation: In Hamming code, parity bits occupy positions that are powers of 2: positions 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, etc. Each parity bit covers specific data bit positions â€” parity bit at position 2^i covers all positions whose binary representation has bit i set. This arrangement allows the error syndrome (XOR of all failing parity checks) to directly point to the error position.

q: For a data word of 4 bits, how many parity bits are required in Hamming code?
explanation: The Hamming bound requires r parity bits to satisfy: 2^r >= m + r + 1, where m is data bits. For m=4: try r=3: 2^3=8 >= 4+3+1=8. So 3 parity bits are needed. The codeword length is 4+3=7 bits (Hamming(7,4)). Positions 1, 2, 4 hold parity bits; positions 3, 5, 6, 7 hold data bits.

q: In Stop-and-Wait ARQ, the sender window size and receiver window size are respectively:
explanation: In Stop-and-Wait ARQ, both sender and receiver window sizes are 1. The sender transmits one frame, then waits for an acknowledgment (ACK) before sending the next. If no ACK arrives within a timeout, the frame is retransmitted. This simplicity comes at the cost of low efficiency, especially on links with high bandwidth-delay product.

q: The efficiency of Stop-and-Wait protocol when propagation delay is 'a' (normalized) is:
explanation: Efficiency of Stop-and-Wait = 1 / (1 + 2a), where a = propagation delay / transmission time. The sender transmits 1 frame (time=1), then waits 2a time (round-trip propagation) for the ACK. Total cycle time = 1 + 2a. Only 1 unit is productive. So efficiency = 1/(1+2a). For large a (satellite links), this is very low, justifying sliding window protocols.

q: In Go-Back-N ARQ with window size W, if a frame is lost or corrupted, the sender retransmits:
options: The lost frame and all subsequent frames up to W frames
The lost frame and all subsequent unacknowledged frames
None; it waits for a NAK
explanation: In Go-Back-N ARQ, if frame i is lost or corrupted, the sender retransmits frame i and ALL subsequent frames that were sent after i (even if correctly received). The receiver discards all out-of-order frames. Window size must be <= 2^n - 1 where n is the number of sequence number bits.

q: What is the maximum sender window size for Go-Back-N protocol using 3-bit sequence numbers?
explanation: For Go-Back-N with n-bit sequence numbers, maximum window size = 2^n - 1. With 3-bit sequence numbers, sequence numbers range from 0 to 7 (8 values), so maximum window size = 2^3 - 1 = 7. The window must be less than 2^n to avoid ambiguity between new frames and retransmissions after a full sequence number wrap-around.

q: In Selective Repeat ARQ, the maximum sender window size with n-bit sequence numbers is:
explanation: For Selective Repeat ARQ with n-bit sequence numbers, maximum window size = 2^(n-1). This is half the sequence number space. With 3-bit sequence numbers, max window = 2^2 = 4. The constraint exists because the receiver must distinguish new frames from retransmissions; a larger window would make a sequence number X ambiguous between a new frame or a retransmission.

q: Which flow control protocol requires the receiver to buffer out-of-order frames?
explanation: Selective Repeat ARQ requires the receiver to buffer out-of-order frames. When a frame is lost, only that specific frame is retransmitted. The receiver accepts and buffers frames arriving out of order (within its window), then delivers them in sequence once the missing frame arrives. Go-Back-N discards all out-of-order frames. Stop-and-Wait sends one frame at a time so ordering is trivial.

q: CSMA/CD stands for:
options: Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection
Channel Sharing Multiple Access / Collision Detection
Carrier Sense Media Access / Carrier Detection
Channel Sense Multiple Access / Carrier Detection
explanation: CSMA/CD stands for Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. 'Carrier Sense' means a station listens before transmitting; 'Multiple Access' means multiple stations share the channel; 'Collision Detection' means a station detects collisions during transmission and aborts immediately, then uses binary exponential backoff. CSMA/CD is used in wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3).

q: In CSMA/CD, after detecting a collision, a station waits for a random backoff time chosen from [0, 2^k - 1] slot times, where k is:
options: The number of stations on the network
explanation: CSMA/CD uses binary exponential backoff. After the k-th collision, the station waits a random number of slot times from [0, 2^k - 1], where k is capped at 10. After 16 consecutive collisions, the transmission is aborted and an error is reported. This exponential growth in the backoff range reduces repeated collisions under heavy load.

q: CSMA/CA is used in which type of network?
explanation: CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) is used in wireless LANs (IEEE 802.11 â€” Wi-Fi). Collision detection (CD) is impractical in wireless because a transmitting node cannot simultaneously listen for collisions. Instead, CSMA/CA uses IFS (Inter-Frame Spacing), random backoff, and optional RTS/CTS to avoid collisions before they happen.

q: In Pure ALOHA, the maximum channel efficiency is approximately:
explanation: Pure ALOHA achieves maximum throughput of 1/(2e) â‰ˆ 18.4%. Stations transmit whenever they have data; if a collision occurs, they wait a random time and retransmit. A frame at time t can collide with frames in the vulnerable period [t-T, t+T] (2T window). Slotted ALOHA halves the vulnerable period, doubling efficiency to 1/e â‰ˆ 36.8%.

q: Slotted ALOHA improves over Pure ALOHA because:
options: It reduces the vulnerable period from 2T to T
explanation: Slotted ALOHA requires all stations to synchronize to time slots and transmit only at the start of a slot. This reduces the vulnerable period from 2T (Pure ALOHA) to T (one slot), halving collision probability. Maximum throughput: Slotted = 1/e â‰ˆ 36.8%, Pure = 1/(2e) â‰ˆ 18.4%. The tradeoff is the need for global time synchronization.

q: A standard Ethernet MAC address is how many bytes long?
explanation: An Ethernet MAC address is 48 bits = 6 bytes, typically written as 6 pairs of hex digits (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E). The first 3 bytes form the OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) assigned by IEEE to manufacturers. The last 3 bytes are assigned by the manufacturer to uniquely identify each network interface card.

q: In a MAC address, the least significant bit of the first byte indicates:
options: Whether it is a unicast or multicast address
Whether it is locally or globally administered
The speed of the network interface
explanation: The LSB of the first byte is the I/G (Individual/Group) bit: 0 = unicast (single device), 1 = multicast/broadcast (group). The broadcast address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF has all bits set to 1. The second LSB is the U/L (Universal/Local) bit: 0 = globally unique (IEEE-assigned), 1 = locally administered.

q: Which Ethernet standard specifies operation at 10 Mbps over twisted pair (10BASE-T)?
explanation: IEEE 802.3 is the original Ethernet standard covering 10 Mbps, including 10BASE-T (twisted pair), 10BASE-2 (thin coax), and 10BASE-5 (thick coax). IEEE 802.3u covers Fast Ethernet (100 Mbps). IEEE 802.3ab covers Gigabit Ethernet over twisted pair (1000BASE-T). IEEE 802.11 is the Wi-Fi wireless standard, not Ethernet.

q: The minimum frame size in Ethernet is 64 bytes. This is required to ensure:
options: Collision detection works correctly before transmission ends
Frames can carry at least 46 bytes of payload
ARP requests fit in a single frame
explanation: Ethernet's 64-byte minimum frame size ensures CSMA/CD works correctly. In the worst case, a collision signal must reach the sender before it finishes transmitting. The round-trip propagation time yields slot time = 51.2 Î¼s at 10 Mbps = 512 bits = 64 bytes. A shorter frame might finish before a collision is detected. Minimum payload = 46 bytes (64 minus 18 bytes of header/trailer overhead).

q: The sliding window protocol improves efficiency over Stop-and-Wait by:
options: Allowing multiple unacknowledged frames in transit
Eliminating the need for sequence numbers
explanation: Sliding window protocols allow the sender to transmit multiple frames without waiting for each ACK individually. The window is the set of frames that can be outstanding at any time. As ACKs arrive, the window slides forward. Efficiency = W/(1+2a) when W < 1+2a, or 1 when W >= 1+2a, where a = propagation delay / transmission time.

q: In Go-Back-N, what happens when the receiver gets a frame with an error?
options: It sends a NAK for that frame and buffers subsequent frames
It discards the erroneous frame and all subsequent frames, sending ACK only for the last correct frame
It corrects the error using FEC and sends ACK
explanation: In Go-Back-N, when the receiver gets an erroneous or out-of-order frame, it discards it and all subsequent frames. It continues sending ACK for the last correctly received in-order frame. The sender eventually times out and retransmits from the lost/errored frame onwards. The receiver window size is 1, so it cannot buffer out-of-order frames.

q: Which characteristic of CSMA/CA (IEEE 802.11) helps solve the hidden node problem?
options: Stations transmit immediately after detecting the channel idle
Stations use RTS/CTS frames to reserve the channel
Collision detection is used to abort ongoing transmissions
Token passing is used to control channel access
explanation: IEEE 802.11 CSMA/CA optionally uses RTS (Request To Send) and CTS (Clear To Send) frames. A sends RTS to AP; AP broadcasts CTS heard by all stations including hidden nodes; hidden nodes set their NAV (Network Allocation Vector) and defer. This virtual carrier sensing avoids collisions at the AP caused by hidden nodes that cannot hear each other.

q: The 'hidden node problem' in wireless networks refers to:
options: A node hidden from the AP due to encryption
Two nodes that can both reach an AP but cannot hear each other, causing collisions at the AP
A node that hides its MAC address using spoofing
A node that is outside the network range
explanation: The hidden node problem: stations A and C both communicate with AP B but are out of each other's radio range. A cannot detect C's transmission, so both may transmit simultaneously, causing a collision at B. RTS/CTS solves this: A sends RTS to B; B responds with CTS heard by both A and C; C defers. Pure CSMA cannot address this problem in wireless environments.

q: Which error detection method provides the STRONGEST error detection capability?
explanation: CRC provides the strongest error detection. CRC-32 (used in Ethernet) detects all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors, all odd numbers of errors, all burst errors of length <= 32 bits, and most longer burst errors. Checksum detects many but misses some patterns. 2D parity detects most multi-bit errors. Single parity detects only odd numbers of errors. CRC's polynomial mathematics gives superior burst error detection.

q: In Hamming code, what does the 'syndrome' represent?
options: The data bits without parity bits
The binary value of XOR-based parity checks, pointing to the error bit position
The generator polynomial used for encoding
The frame check sequence appended to the frame
explanation: The syndrome is computed at the receiver by performing each parity check. If all checks pass, syndrome = 0 (no error). If an error exists, the syndrome's binary value equals the bit position of the error. For example, syndrome 011 (binary 3) means bit position 3 is erroneous. This allows Hamming code to both detect and correct single-bit errors without retransmission.

q: The CRC-CCITT generator polynomial x^16 + x^12 + x^5 + 1 produces how many check bits?
explanation: The degree of the CRC generator polynomial equals the number of check bits appended. CRC-CCITT uses x^16 + x^12 + x^5 + 1, which has degree 16, producing 16 check bits (2 bytes). CRC-32 uses a degree-32 polynomial, producing 32 check bits (4 bytes). Higher degree means better error detection capability and more overhead.

q: In Ethernet II framing, the 'EtherType' field identifies:
options: The upper-layer protocol being carried (e.g., 0x0800 for IPv4)
explanation: In Ethernet II (DIX Ethernet) frames, the 2-byte EtherType field identifies the upper-layer network protocol. Key values: 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x0806 = ARP, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x8100 = VLAN-tagged (802.1Q). In IEEE 802.3 frames, this field is a Length field (values <= 1500); LLC/SNAP headers then identify the upper protocol. EtherType values > 1536 distinguish Ethernet II from 802.3.

q: What is the purpose of the preamble in an Ethernet frame?
options: To identify the destination MAC address
To synchronize the receiver's clock with the sender's clock
explanation: The Ethernet preamble consists of 7 bytes of alternating 1s and 0s (10101010...) followed by 1 byte SFD (Start Frame Delimiter: 10101011). It allows receiving stations to synchronize their bit-timing clock with the incoming signal. The SFD's ending '11' pattern signals the start of the actual frame. The preamble/SFD are not included in the 64-byte minimum frame size calculation.

q: Which statement about Selective Repeat ARQ is TRUE?
options: The receiver window size is always 1
Only the specific erroneous or lost frame is retransmitted
The sender retransmits all frames from the error onwards
It requires no buffering at the receiver
explanation: In Selective Repeat ARQ, only the specific lost or errored frame is retransmitted. The receiver buffers correctly-received out-of-order frames within its window, delivering them in order once the missing frame arrives. This is more bandwidth-efficient than Go-Back-N but requires more receiver buffer memory. Both sender and receiver window sizes equal 2^(n-1) for n-bit sequence numbers.

q: The throughput efficiency of Go-Back-N (window size W, normalized delay a) when W < 1+2a is:
explanation: For Go-Back-N: if W >= (1+2a), efficiency = 1 (full utilization â€” pipe stays full). If W < (1+2a), efficiency = W/(1+2a) â€” the sender runs out of window space before the first ACK returns. Stop-and-Wait is the special case W=1, giving 1/(1+2a). This formula shows that large windows are essential on high-latency links such as satellite connections.

q: In ALOHA systems, the throughput S in Pure ALOHA as a function of offered load G is:
explanation: Pure ALOHA throughput S = G * e^(-2G), where G is the total offered load. A frame succeeds if no other frame overlaps its 2T vulnerable period: P(success) = e^(-2G). Maximizing S: G=0.5 gives S_max = 0.5*e^(-1) = 1/(2e) â‰ˆ 18.4%. Slotted ALOHA: S = G*e^(-G), max at G=1, S_max = 1/e â‰ˆ 36.8%.

q: Which MAC protocol is used in traditional Ethernet with shared coaxial cable?
explanation: Traditional Ethernet (10BASE-2 thin coax, 10BASE-5 thick coax) uses CSMA/CD. Stations sense carrier before transmitting, detect collisions by monitoring the line during transmission, and use binary exponential backoff for retransmission. Modern switched Ethernet uses full-duplex point-to-point links where collisions cannot occur, making CSMA/CD unnecessary in practice.

q: The Ethernet slot time (51.2 Î¼s for 10 Mbps) is designed to be at least:
options: Twice the maximum end-to-end propagation delay (round-trip time)
The time to transmit a 1500-byte frame
explanation: The Ethernet slot time must be at least twice the maximum end-to-end propagation delay (round-trip time). This ensures that a collision signal reaches the sending station before it finishes transmitting the minimum-size frame. At 10 Mbps: 51.2 Î¼s * 10 Mbps = 512 bits = 64 bytes = minimum Ethernet frame size. This is why minimum frame size and slot time are directly linked.

q: The Ethernet broadcast MAC address is:
explanation: The Ethernet broadcast address is FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (all 48 bits = 1). Every network interface on the LAN segment receives and processes broadcast frames. The all-zeros address (00:00:00:00:00:00) is invalid as a source. Addresses starting with 01:00:5E map to IPv4 multicast groups. Broadcast is a special multicast where the I/G bit (LSB of first byte) = 1.

q: Which protocol uses a circulating 'token' to grant channel access in a ring topology?
explanation: Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) uses token passing for deterministic channel access. A special bit pattern (token) circulates around the ring. A station transmits only when it captures the token, then releases it after sending. This eliminates collisions entirely and provides bounded access delay â€” useful for real-time applications. Token Bus (IEEE 802.4) applies the same concept on a bus topology.

q: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) at the Data Link Layer is used to:
options: Resolve domain names to IP addresses
Resolve IP addresses to MAC addresses
Assign IP addresses dynamically to hosts
Detect duplicate IP addresses on the network
explanation: ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC (hardware) addresses on a local network segment. When host A needs to reach IP address B but lacks B's MAC, A broadcasts an ARP request. The host owning IP B replies with its MAC address. A caches this mapping in its ARP table. ARP operates at the boundary of the Network Layer (IP addresses) and Data Link Layer (MAC addresses).

q: Which correctly describes the retransmission difference between Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat?
options: Go-Back-N retransmits only the lost frame; Selective Repeat retransmits from the lost frame onwards
Go-Back-N retransmits from the lost frame onwards; Selective Repeat retransmits only the lost frame
Both retransmit only the lost frame but differ in window size
Both retransmit all frames from the lost one but differ in receiver buffering
explanation: Go-Back-N retransmits the lost/errored frame AND all subsequent frames sent after it (going back). Selective Repeat retransmits ONLY the specific lost/errored frame. Go-Back-N: simpler receiver (window=1, no buffering) but wastes bandwidth retransmitting good frames. Selective Repeat: efficient but needs receiver buffering. Window sizes: GBN max = 2^n - 1, SR max = 2^(n-1).

q: CRC-32 (used in Ethernet) can detect all burst errors of length up to how many bits?
explanation: A CRC with generator polynomial of degree r detects ALL burst errors of length <= r. CRC-32 has degree 32, so it detects all burst errors of 32 bits or fewer. It also detects all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors, all odd-bit errors (since x+1 divides the polynomial), and burst errors of length 33 with probability approximately 1 - 2^(-32). This makes CRC-32 extremely reliable for Ethernet frame integrity.

q: In IEEE 802.3 Ethernet, the Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) is:
options: A field added inside the frame to indicate boundaries
The mandatory minimum idle time between consecutive frame transmissions
The synchronization gap within the preamble
The time allocated for binary exponential backoff
explanation: The Inter-Frame Gap (IFG) is the mandatory minimum idle time between consecutive Ethernet frame transmissions: 9.6 Î¼s at 10 Mbps (96 bit times). It gives receiving stations time to process the incoming frame and prepare for the next. The IFG also prevents a single station from monopolizing the medium with back-to-back frames and helps stations distinguish consecutive frames.

q: Which OSI layer handles framing, MAC addressing, and error detection but NOT routing?
explanation: The Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2) is responsible for framing (encapsulating bits into frames), MAC addressing (physical LAN delivery), error detection (CRC/parity), and flow control on a single link segment. Routing across networks is a Network Layer (Layer 3) function. The DLL is subdivided into LLC (Logical Link Control) and MAC (Media Access Control) sublayers per IEEE 802 standards.

q: How many redundant parity bits does Hamming code require for a 7-bit data word to enable single-bit error correction?
explanation: Hamming bound: 2^r >= m + r + 1. For m=7 data bits, try r=3: 2^3=8 < 7+3+1=11 (fails). Try r=4: 2^4=16 >= 7+4+1=12 (passes). So 4 parity bits are required, producing an 11-bit codeword. Compare: for m=4 data bits, r=3 suffices (Hamming(7,4)). The number of required parity bits grows as log2 of the data size.

q: In CSMA, '1-persistent' means the station:
options: Retransmits exactly once after a collision
Continuously monitors the channel and transmits immediately when idle (probability 1)
Uses a token with probability 1 before transmitting
Increases window size by 1 after each successful frame
explanation: 1-persistent CSMA: if the channel is busy, the station continuously senses and transmits immediately when it becomes idle (with probability 1). This is aggressive and causes many collisions under heavy load. p-persistent CSMA transmits with probability p when idle (waits one slot with probability 1-p), balancing load. Non-persistent CSMA waits a random time before re-sensing when busy, reducing collisions but increasing delay.

q: The maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA (~36.8%) occurs when offered load G equals:
explanation: Slotted ALOHA throughput S = G * e^(-G). Differentiating: dS/dG = e^(-G)(1 - G) = 0, so G = 1 for maximum. At G=1: S_max = 1 * e^(-1) = 1/e â‰ˆ 0.368 = 36.8%. For Pure ALOHA (S = G*e^(-2G)), maximum occurs at G=0.5, giving S_max = 1/(2e) â‰ˆ 18.4%. The G=1 optimum means exactly one average transmission attempt per time slot.

q: Which is the correct field order in a standard Ethernet II frame (excluding preamble)?
options: Destination MAC | Source MAC | EtherType | Data | FCS
Source MAC | Destination MAC | EtherType | Data | FCS
Destination MAC | EtherType | Source MAC | Data | FCS
EtherType | Destination MAC | Source MAC | Data | FCS
explanation: Ethernet II frame structure (after preamble+SFD): Destination MAC (6 bytes) | Source MAC (6 bytes) | EtherType (2 bytes) | Data/Payload (46-1500 bytes) | FCS/CRC-32 (4 bytes). Total: 64-1518 bytes. The FCS covers everything from Destination MAC through Data. The preamble (7B) + SFD (1B) precede this and are stripped before the frame is processed by the Data Link Layer.

q: How many bits are in an IPv4 address?
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits long, written as four octets in dotted-decimal notation (e.g., 192.168.1.1). IPv6 uses 128 bits.

q: Which class of IPv4 address has the first octet range of 192 to 223?
explanation: Class C addresses range from 192.0.0.0 to 223.255.255.255. Class A: 1â€“126, Class B: 128â€“191, Class D: 224â€“239 (multicast).

q: What is the default subnet mask for a Class B IP address?
explanation: Class B (128.0.0.0â€“191.255.255.255) has a default subnet mask of 255.255.0.0 (/16), giving 65,534 usable host addresses per network.

q: CIDR notation 192.168.10.0/26 provides how many usable host addresses?
explanation: /26 means 26 bits for network, 6 bits for hosts. 2^6 = 64 total addresses; subtract 2 (network + broadcast) = 62 usable hosts.

q: Which IPv4 address range is reserved for APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing)?
explanation: APIPA uses 169.254.0.0/16. When a DHCP client cannot obtain an address, it self-assigns an address in this range to allow local communication.

q: What is the loopback address in IPv4?
explanation: 127.0.0.1 is the loopback address used to test the TCP/IP stack on a local machine. Traffic sent here never leaves the host.

q: How many bits constitute an IPv6 address?
explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits long, written as eight groups of four hexadecimal digits separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0db8::1).

q: Which IPv6 address type is equivalent to IPv4 broadcast?
options: There is no broadcast in IPv6
explanation: IPv6 does not support broadcast. It uses multicast instead. The all-nodes multicast address ff02::1 serves a similar purpose to limited broadcast.

q: What does NAT stand for in networking?
explanation: NAT (Network Address Translation) maps private IP addresses to a public IP address, conserving IPv4 address space and providing a basic security layer.

q: Which type of NAT maps one private IP address to one public IP address on a one-to-one basis?
explanation: Static NAT creates a permanent one-to-one mapping between a private IP and a public IP. Dynamic NAT uses a pool, and PAT maps multiple privates to one public using port numbers.

q: What is the purpose of ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)?
options: Resolve domain names to IP addresses
Resolve IP addresses to MAC addresses
Translate private IPs to public IPs
explanation: ARP resolves a known IPv4 address to its corresponding MAC (physical) address on the same local network segment by broadcasting a request.

q: RARP (Reverse ARP) is used to:
explanation: RARP allows a diskless machine to discover its IP address by broadcasting its MAC address. A RARP server replies with the assigned IP. RARP is largely replaced by DHCP.

q: ICMP operates at which layer of the OSI model?
explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) operates at the Network Layer (Layer 3). It is encapsulated in IP datagrams and used for error reporting and diagnostic functions.

q: Which ICMP message type is used by the 'ping' utility?
explanation: Ping uses ICMP Echo Request (Type 8) and Echo Reply (Type 0) messages to test reachability and measure round-trip time to a host.

q: The 'traceroute' utility uses which ICMP message to identify hops?
explanation: Traceroute sends packets with incrementing TTL values. Each router decrements TTL; when TTL=0, it sends ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) back to the source, revealing that hop.

q: Which routing algorithm is used by RIP (Routing Information Protocol)?
explanation: RIP uses the Distance Vector algorithm (Bellman-Ford). Each router shares its routing table with directly connected neighbors periodically (every 30 seconds).

q: What is the maximum hop count supported by RIP version 1 and 2?
explanation: RIP supports a maximum hop count of 15. A hop count of 16 is considered infinite (unreachable), limiting RIP to small networks.

q: OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) uses which routing algorithm?
explanation: OSPF uses the Link State algorithm. Each router floods Link State Advertisements (LSAs) to build a complete topology map, then runs Dijkstra's Shortest Path First algorithm.

q: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is classified as which type of routing protocol?
options: Interior Gateway Protocol using Distance Vector
Interior Gateway Protocol using Link State
Exterior Gateway Protocol using Path Vector
Exterior Gateway Protocol using Distance Vector
explanation: BGP is an Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP) that uses the Path Vector algorithm. It routes between Autonomous Systems (ASes) on the Internet and is the Internet's core routing protocol.

q: Which metric does OSPF use to calculate the best path?
explanation: OSPF uses cost as its metric, which is inversely proportional to bandwidth (Cost = Reference bandwidth / Interface bandwidth). Lower cost = better path.

q: What is the administrative distance of RIP in Cisco IOS?
explanation: Cisco IOS assigns RIP an administrative distance of 120. OSPF = 110, EIGRP internal = 90, BGP (eBGP) = 20. Lower AD is more trustworthy.

q: In OSPF, what is the term for the router elected to represent a multi-access network segment?
explanation: On multi-access networks (like Ethernet), OSPF elects a Designated Router (DR) and Backup DR (BDR) to reduce LSA flooding. All other routers form adjacencies with the DR/BDR only.

q: Which subnet mask in CIDR notation represents a host route?
explanation: A /32 subnet mask (255.255.255.255) represents a single host route â€” it matches exactly one IP address. /30 gives 2 usable hosts, /31 is used for point-to-point links.

q: The IP address 172.20.0.0/12 belongs to which private address range?
How many subnets are created by borrowing 3 bits from the host portion?
explanation: Borrowing n bits from the host portion creates 2^n subnets. With 3 bits: 2^3 = 8 subnets. Each subnet has fewer host addresses to accommodate the additional network bits.

q: Which congestion control mechanism is used by TCP to reduce the sending rate when congestion is detected?
explanation: TCP uses Slow Start (exponential growth from 1 MSS), then Congestion Avoidance (linear growth). On packet loss, it halves the congestion window (AIMD) and may restart Slow Start.

q: What does the TTL field in an IP header prevent?
explanation: TTL (Time To Live) is decremented by 1 at each router. When TTL reaches 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent, preventing packets from looping forever.

q: What is the size of the IP header without any options?
explanation: The minimum IPv4 header size is 20 bytes (160 bits). The IHL (Internet Header Length) field indicates the header length in 32-bit words; minimum value is 5 (5 Ã— 4 = 20 bytes).

q: Which field in the IPv4 header is used to reassemble fragmented packets?
explanation: The Identification field contains the same value for all fragments of the same original datagram, allowing the destination to group fragments for reassembly using the Fragment Offset.

q: IPv6 uses which mechanism instead of ARP for address resolution?
explanation: IPv6 replaces ARP with NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol), which uses ICMPv6 messages (Neighbor Solicitation and Neighbor Advertisement) for address resolution and router discovery.

q: What is the IPv6 equivalent of the IPv4 loopback address 127.0.0.1?
explanation: ::1 is the IPv6 loopback address (equivalent to 127.0.0.1). It is the compressed form of 0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001.

q: Which IPv6 address type starts with FE80::/10 and is valid only within a single network segment?
explanation: Link-Local addresses (FE80::/10) are automatically configured on every IPv6-enabled interface and are only routable within the local link/segment. They are mandatory for IPv6 operation.

q: In distance vector routing, the 'count-to-infinity' problem is solved by which technique?
explanation: Split Horizon prevents a router from advertising a route back on the interface it was learned from, helping to avoid routing loops and the count-to-infinity problem in distance vector protocols.

q: What is the hello interval used by OSPF on broadcast networks?
explanation: OSPF uses a Hello interval of 10 seconds on broadcast and point-to-point networks (40 seconds on NBMA). The dead interval is typically 4Ã— the hello interval (40 seconds).

q: Which routing protocol uses AS_PATH attribute to prevent routing loops?
explanation: BGP uses the AS_PATH attribute, which lists all ASes a route has traversed. If a router sees its own AS number in the AS_PATH, it discards the route to prevent loops.

q: Port Address Translation (PAT) is also known as:
explanation: PAT (Port Address Translation), also called NAT Overload, maps multiple private IP addresses to a single public IP using different port numbers, maximizing IPv4 address conservation.

q: What is the network address of 192.168.5.130/25?
explanation: /25 means 255.255.255.128. The subnet boundary is every 128 addresses: 0â€“127 and 128â€“255. Host .130 falls in the second subnet, so network address = 192.168.5.128.

q: Which ICMP message is sent by a router when it cannot forward a packet?
explanation: ICMP Destination Unreachable (Type 3) is sent when a router or host cannot deliver a packet. Various codes specify the reason (network unreachable, host unreachable, port unreachable, etc.).

q: What is the broadcast address for the network 10.0.0.0/8?
explanation: For 10.0.0.0/8 (mask 255.0.0.0), the host portion is 24 bits. Setting all host bits to 1 gives 10.255.255.255 as the broadcast address.

q: Which algorithm does RIP use, and what is its fundamental equation?
explanation: RIP uses the Bellman-Ford (distance vector) algorithm. The equation D(x,y) = min over neighbors v of {c(x,v) + D(v,y)} calculates the shortest path to destination y from node x via neighbor v.

q: In IPv4, what does the 'DF' (Don't Fragment) bit in the Flags field indicate?
options: The packet is the last fragment
The packet must not be fragmented
explanation: The DF bit instructs routers not to fragment the packet. If the packet exceeds the MTU and DF is set, the router drops it and sends an ICMP 'Fragmentation Needed' message back to the source.

q: VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking) allows:
options: All subnets to use the same mask
Different subnets to use different prefix lengths
explanation: VLSM allows a network to be divided into subnets of different sizes by using different prefix lengths, enabling efficient IP address allocation matching actual host requirements per subnet.

q: Which field in an IPv6 header replaces the IPv4 TTL field?
explanation: IPv6 uses a Hop Limit field (8 bits) instead of IPv4's TTL. It serves the same purpose â€” decremented by 1 at each router, and the packet is discarded when it reaches 0.

q: How many addresses does a /30 subnet provide, and how many are usable for hosts?
explanation: A /30 subnet has 32-30=2 host bits, giving 2^2=4 total addresses. Subtracting the network address and broadcast address leaves 2 usable host addresses, ideal for point-to-point links.

q: Which protocol is used to detect and report errors in IPv6 networks, replacing ICMPv4?
explanation: ICMPv6 handles error reporting and diagnostics in IPv6, similar to ICMPv4. It also incorporates NDP functions (Neighbor Solicitation/Advertisement) that were separate protocols in IPv4.

q: The 'leaky bucket' algorithm for congestion control:
options: Allows bursting up to a maximum rate
Outputs packets at a constant rate regardless of input burst
explanation: The Leaky Bucket algorithm outputs traffic at a constant rate, smoothing bursty input. Excess packets overflow (are dropped) if the bucket is full, enforcing a strict output rate.

q: Which BGP attribute is used first in the BGP best-path selection process?
explanation: In Cisco BGP, Weight (Cisco-proprietary) is checked first; highest wins. Then Local Preference (highest), then AS_PATH length (shortest), then MED (lowest), then eBGP over iBGP.

q: What is the function of the OSPF Area Border Router (ABR)?
options: Connect OSPF to other routing domains (BGP/EIGRP)
Connect multiple OSPF areas to Area 0 (backbone)
Elect the Designated Router in multi-access networks
explanation: An ABR (Area Border Router) connects one or more OSPF areas to the backbone (Area 0). It maintains a separate LSDB for each area and summarizes routes between areas.

q: Supernetting (route aggregation) in CIDR combines multiple networks by:
explanation: Supernetting decreases the prefix length (fewer network bits) to aggregate multiple contiguous networks into a single summary route, reducing routing table size. E.g., four /24s can become one /22.

q: In the context of IPv4 addressing, what is the purpose of the subnet mask?
options: Distinguish the network portion from the host portion
explanation: The subnet mask is ANDed with an IP address to extract the network portion. Bits set to 1 represent network bits; bits set to 0 represent host bits, enabling routers to make forwarding decisions.

q: Which private IP range defined by RFC 1918 provides the largest number of addresses?
explanation: 10.0.0.0/8 provides 2^24 - 2 = 16,777,214 usable addresses, the largest of the RFC 1918 ranges. 172.16.0.0/12 provides ~1M and 192.168.0.0/16 provides ~65K usable addresses.

q: What happens when a router receives a packet with TTL = 1?
options: Decrements TTL to 0, discards the packet, and sends ICMP Time Exceeded
Sends the packet to the default gateway
explanation: The router decrements TTL from 1 to 0, discards the packet, and sends an ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11, Code 0) message to the source. This is the mechanism traceroute uses to map network paths.

q: Which transport layer protocol provides connection-oriented, reliable data transfer?
explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is connection-oriented and provides reliable, ordered, and error-checked delivery of data. UDP is connectionless and unreliable.

q: Which transport layer protocol is best suited for real-time applications like video streaming and online gaming?
explanation: UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is preferred for real-time applications because it has lower latency and overhead. It does not retransmit lost packets, which is acceptable when timeliness matters more than reliability.

q: What is the correct sequence of messages in a TCP 3-way handshake?
explanation: TCP 3-way handshake: (1) Client sends SYN to server, (2) Server responds with SYN-ACK, (3) Client sends ACK. This establishes a full-duplex connection.

q: During a TCP 3-way handshake, which side initiates the connection?
explanation: In TCP, the client initiates the connection by sending the first SYN segment to the server. The server must be in LISTEN state to accept incoming connections.

q: How many messages are exchanged during a TCP 4-way connection termination?
explanation: TCP uses 4-way termination: FIN from initiator, ACK from receiver, FIN from receiver, ACK from initiator. Each side closes independently, so 4 messages are needed.

q: What is the purpose of the TIME_WAIT state in TCP?
options: To wait for the server to close
To ensure the last ACK is received and old duplicate packets expire
explanation: TIME_WAIT state (lasting 2*MSL) ensures the final ACK reaches the remote side and any duplicate packets from previous connections expire before a new connection uses the same socket pair.

q: Which flag is set in the TCP segment to initiate connection termination?
explanation: The FIN (Finish) flag is used to signal that the sender has finished sending data and wishes to close its half of the TCP connection. The connection termination requires FIN from both sides.

q: What does TCP's flow control mechanism prevent?
explanation: TCP flow control prevents the sender from overwhelming the receiver's buffer. The receiver advertises its receive window size (rwnd) so the sender limits the amount of unacknowledged data in flight.

q: Which TCP field is used to implement flow control?
explanation: The Window Size field in the TCP header contains the receive window (rwnd) advertised by the receiver. The sender must not send more unacknowledged bytes than this window allows.

q: In TCP's slow start algorithm, how does the congestion window (cwnd) grow initially?
explanation: During slow start, cwnd starts at 1 MSS and doubles every RTT (increases by 1 MSS for each ACK received), resulting in exponential growth until ssthresh is reached or packet loss occurs.

q: What happens to the congestion window in TCP when the slow start threshold (ssthresh) is reached?
explanation: When cwnd reaches ssthresh, TCP transitions from slow start to congestion avoidance, where cwnd grows linearly by 1 MSS per RTT instead of doubling.

q: In TCP Reno, when a timeout (packet loss) is detected, what happens to ssthresh and cwnd?
explanation: On timeout in TCP Reno: ssthresh is set to cwnd/2, and cwnd is reset to 1 MSS. Slow start restarts from 1 MSS up to the new ssthresh.

q: What is the well-known port number for HTTP?
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) uses port 80 by default. HTTPS uses port 443, FTP uses port 21, and SMTP uses port 25.

q: Which port number is used by HTTPS?
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443. It is HTTP over TLS/SSL for encrypted communication.

q: What is the port number used by DNS?
explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) uses port 53 for both UDP (standard queries) and TCP (zone transfers or large responses).

q: Which port does FTP use for control connections?
explanation: FTP uses port 21 for the control connection (commands) and port 20 for data transfer in active mode.

q: What is a socket in networking?
options: An endpoint for communication identified by IP address and port number
explanation: A socket is an endpoint for communication, uniquely identified by the combination of an IP address and a port number. It is the interface between the application layer and transport layer.

q: What uniquely identifies a TCP socket (connection)?
options: Source IP + Source Port + Destination IP + Destination Port
explanation: A TCP connection (socket) is uniquely identified by a 4-tuple: source IP, source port, destination IP, and destination port. This is called the socket pair.

q: What is multiplexing in the context of the transport layer?
options: Gathering data from multiple application sockets and passing to network layer with headers
explanation: Multiplexing at the transport layer means gathering data from multiple application sockets, encapsulating them with transport-layer headers (including port numbers), and passing them to the network layer.

q: What is demultiplexing in the transport layer?
options: Sending data to multiple hosts simultaneously
Delivering received segments to the correct application socket based on header fields
explanation: Demultiplexing is the process of examining the destination port (and other fields) in an incoming segment and delivering it to the correct application socket.

q: UDP demultiplexing uses which fields to identify the destination socket?
options: Source IP and source port only
Destination IP and destination port only
Source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port
explanation: UDP demultiplexing uses only the destination IP address and destination port number to identify the socket. Two UDP segments from different sources with the same destination IP and port go to the same socket.
